talk_metadata.talk_title: Virtual patients with multimodal foundation models and agents
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I am still very skeptical because if there's not the signal in H&E, then even a foundation model that has been trained on multiple modalities cannot infer what is simply not present."
Speaker said: "We see that there is a way for those models by the amount of data it sees to distinguish between technical artifact and biological signal."
Speaker said: "We are only at eight cancer types at this point... the diversity here helps us."

key_data_gaps: No clinical validation or prospective trial data presented
No external benchmark against standard of care biomarkers

crispro_opportunity.description: Speaker highlighted that their multimodal foundation model derives biomarkers that outperform existing clinical stratification schemes but no external validation was shown.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "... leads to a better kind of stratification than, again, the original cancer cell study..."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can apply its computational spine to retrospectively validate these AI‑derived signatures across larger, outcome‑annotated cohorts.

crispro_opportunity.description: The need to decide when spatial proteomics is required versus when routine pathology suffices suggests a trial enrichment strategy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Do we always need a spatial proteomics screen? No. Many decisions can be simply made from routine pathology."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model assay selection as an optimization problem, recommending minimal panels for prospective biomarker trials.

cited_competitors.name: Meta vision transformers
cited_competitors.context: Referenced as the architecture adapted for histopathology foundation models

cited_competitors.name: Existing virtual staining methods
cited_competitors.context: Compared unfavorably to multimodal models for virtual staining tasks

unresolved_questions: How will the models perform on truly unseen cancer types and modalities?
What is the minimal data requirement to achieve robust clinical decision support?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Generative models for disease dynamics and scientific reasoning
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Flow matching is behind a lot of the state-of-the-art visual generation models today."
Speaker said: "We still see performance gets worse the more out of distribution you are, but I think it's still overall promising trajectories here."
Speaker said: "The actual flow matching is done in the latent space."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Limited ability to model discrete biological events such as apoptosis within a continuous latent trajectory

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker noted that the method still suffers from shortcut learning and out‑of‑distribution degradation, indicating potential exploitable weaknesses.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on large matched imaging‑transcriptomics datasets that are currently scarce.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that the model can generate smooth continuous trajectories (observation) but then admitted that modeling discrete events like apoptosis remains a challenge (contradiction).

key_data_gaps: No large-scale matched multimodal (image + transcriptomics) training set demonstrated
Limited evaluation on truly unseen perturbations

crispro_opportunity.description: Speaker identified difficulty modeling discrete events (e.g., apoptosis) with continuous flow models.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "...how do you model some discrete transitions, like the apoptosis, and other biological process?"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can incorporate explicit mechanistic graphs to augment continuous latent flows, enabling discrete event modeling.

crispro_opportunity.description: CellFlux performance degrades out‑of‑distribution, suggesting a need for robustness improvements.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "...performance gets worse the more out of distribution you are..."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s uncertainty quantification can flag OOD cases and trigger fallback models.

crispro_opportunity.description: Early‑fusion multimodal architecture outperforms late‑fusion, but still suffers shortcut learning.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We compare this to late fusion schemes... they actually don't learn how to connect the information between modalities."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can provide a unified multimodal embedding that is explicitly regularized against shortcut features.

cited_competitors.name: CellFlux V2
cited_competitors.context: Discussed as the current state of the flow‑matching model

cited_competitors.name: MicroVQA benchmark
cited_competitors.context: Used to evaluate visual question answering models

unresolved_questions: How can flow‑matching be extended to hierarchical modeling of cell populations?
What are optimal loss functions for multimodal joint training beyond next‑token prediction?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Reliable medical AI: agents that plan, evaluate, and verify
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Many systems drop drastically in performance if they have to actually do the workup on their own."
Speaker said: "We need to develop new methods, new strategies to ground and verify outputs of AI systems."
Speaker said: "We propose a process reward model that can check each reasoning step against literature and guidelines."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of grounding in structured medical knowledge leading to hallucinations

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker noted that the atlas is a re‑structured representation of existing literature, not novel IP, suggesting easy replication.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on static QA pairs may limit adaptability to emerging guidelines.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented a vision of fully reliable agents (observation) but simultaneously admitted that current models still hallucinate and need step‑wise verification (contradiction).

key_data_gaps: No prospective clinical evaluation of the agentic system
Limited public benchmarks for multimodal medical reasoning

crispro_opportunity.description: Need for structured, step‑wise verification suggests designing trials that capture intermediate reasoning checkpoints.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We try to disentangle those two tasks and have some kind of policing language model... that can analyze each individual statement."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can generate and validate reasoning traces, providing measurable intermediate endpoints for trials.

crispro_opportunity.description: Myriad’s structured QA format improves retrieval and reduces hallucination compared to unstructured text.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "What we would like to have is a more organized representation of the data, where there's a neat hierarchy..."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s knowledge graph backbone can be integrated to further enhance structured retrieval.

crispro_opportunity.description: Existing language‑model‑only agents can be retrofitted with CrisPRO’s process‑reward verification layer.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We can improve many different language model by just hook plugging it in at inference time."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO provides a plug‑in verification module that can be attached to legacy agents.

cited_competitors.name: GPT‑5 / Gemini‑3 Pro
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as state‑of‑the‑art models evaluated on MicroVQA

cited_competitors.name: Standard RAG pipelines
cited_competitors.context: Contrasted with the Myriad structured knowledge approach

unresolved_questions: How will step‑wise verification scale to real‑time clinical workflows?
What metrics best capture the trustworthiness of an agent’s reasoning trace?
